# Core Contracting Principles

| Principle | Rule |
| --- | --- |
| P1. SOW controls delivery risk | Do not let the MSA carry project-specific commitments. Each SOW should define scope, staffing model, rates, utilization, assumptions, dependencies, acceptance, and project-specific deviations. |
| P2. Client cooperation is a condition of performance | Preserve language making Trinetix performance dependent on timely client instructions, access, approvals, documentation, and feedback. |
| P3. IP transfer follows payment | Do not agree that the client acquires deliverable IP before full payment for the relevant services, milestone, or period. |
| P4. Background IP and know-how remain Trinetix assets | Always preserve reusable tools, methods, templates, libraries, frameworks, know-how, skills, experience, and non-client-specific developments. |
| P5. Liability must match deal economics | Keep liability tied to recent fees or the relevant SOW. Escalate uncapped liability, broad exceptions, or customer-specific caps that exceed commercial value. |
| P6. No hidden regulated services | The templates support IT consulting and development services, not regulated legal, accounting, medical, financial, or employment services. |
| P7. AI use must be controlled and disclosed when required | Preserve the right to use GenAI responsibly. |
| P8. Security and privacy obligations must be specific | Security, privacy, HIPAA, and regulated-data obligations should be in an appendix, DPA, BAA, SOW, or security addendum. Avoid broad undefined compliance commitments. |
| P9. External comments must be business-friendly | Use concise and neutral comments. Do not disclose internal risk appetite or escalation thresholds. |
| P10. The AI does not approve contracts | AI may review, summarize, flag, draft fallback wording, and prepare escalation notes. A lawyer must approve all final legal positions. |
